100
    The sketch, which had been done in the 1840s, was clearly intended as a study, and not for public
display. Nevertheless, the art dealer entrusted with the task of auctioning off Ingres’ works was determined
to sell it. Showing some delicacy of his own, the dealer first offered to sell the sketch to Mme. Moitessier’s
husband. But Monsieur Moitessier responded by filing suit, claiming that, as the owner of the portrait of
his wife, he was already the owner of any preparatory sketches. (His wife’s rights as such were not at issue
in nineteenth-century French law.) The issue was thus framed once again as one involving both rights to
property and rights to privacy.

34
          inviolability would be offended if the image of the mère de famille could be

surrendered to the publicity of a banal public auction . . . . 101

The court accordingly ordered the dealer in possession of the sketch to sell it to M.

Moitessier. The same basic analysis was confirmed in yet further high-profile cases over

the following decades, which established the principle that we have a “ ‘sacred and

inalienable right over ourselves,” and consequently over the reproduction of our

image.”102 The most famous of these, in 1900, was the lawsuit between Whistler and the

Baronet Eden, which Whistler himself chronicled in his little screed “The Baronet and the

Butterfly.”103

In part, French privacy law was thus the product of the culture of the Paris art

world, with its nude models, defiant immoralism, and large artistic egos. The cases that

that grew out of that world generally concluded that there was a right to one’s “image”

that was distinct from, and in tension with, rights of property. But that was only one of

the strains in the making of French privacy law. The other had to do with the free press,

and it reflected exactly the same anxieties that had accompanied press liberalization in

1791 and 1819.
